item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The original image belonged to 77krc; however, it has been hidden away from public view and only a select few people are permitted to view it. As such, it is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0, especially with how their other photos are licensed under BY-NC-ND 2.0, and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a pretty big train nerd, so when I saw SCP-1489 years ago, it immediatly became my favorite article on the site, and I'm happy it's still here and doing very well.

I hope you have a wonderful 2020 and beyond.
-Researcher Borsig

item.title: Re: Yep, I'm back.
item.content:encoded: Perhaps 18 carriges is a little excessive for a Minerva tank, but that's just me. Good article.

item.title: Re: I have some criticisms
item.content:encoded: I'm afraid you are mistaken; there is indeed a Minerva-class locomotive. They were designed by Joseph Beattie and built in 1856. 3 were built as 2-4-0T. Same designer, though!

item.title: I have some criticisms
item.content:encoded: I am a researcher of railway history, and I have one major problem. So, you say the engine is a Minerva class tank engine. Well, I’m assuming what you did is you went on google and typed in “British Train Model” and you found something called a Minerva. Well, you shoulda done some fact checking. Because Minerva, is a toy train company. Not a class of locomotive. If you want, I can find some examples of trains from the desired time period for you to use that actually exist and operated for the L&SWR. Might I suggest an L&SWR 0298 class 2-4-0wt well Tank. Built in 1863.

item.title: Well...
item.content:encoded:
That was certainly quite an entertaining read. I've always thought of myself as a bit of a rail enthusiast.

Mind you, after reading Seibai's "clues", I'm still hardly any closer to working out what he intended was the origin for this thing; the only major "accident" involving the L&SWR in his given time period was the Egham Rail Crash of 1864, and even that was a relatively mild incident; 6 fatalities, which is comparatively mild to the 1906/07 series of derailments.

Any of the three 1906/07 derailments would have been a better basis for this SCP; not only were they far more catastrophic events, there is also a huge amount of mystery surrounding these disasters in real life as well; 3 derailments within 16 months, in completely different parts of the country, all at night. All 3 accidents involved an experienced driver, with the involved trains travelling on tight curves that had strict speed limits in place. All 3 disasters resulted in the deaths of the entire driving crew, and none of the 3 disasters have ever been truly, 100% solved with absolute certainty. Grantham in particular since the approach is unmistakable, and Salisbury being the most severe in terms of loss of life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd love to see a reverse version of this. you're just walking through the desert when *splat* you get hit by an invisible train.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last sentence, all on its own, earned my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You stated it perfectly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man… you guys have no idea how sick I am of hearing "but an article NEEDS to have a hook in order to survive!" on so many articles.

This skip, in my opinion, is one of the most perfect examples of how an article doesn't need to have a hook, at least, not how we think of it. The way I see most people using it, a hook would have to be something not directly obvious from the skip yet it latches your attention onto it in order to make it compelling, right? Like a backstory, or an effect of the skip that you only realize the implications of when you dig further, or a GOI connected to it, etc., right?

True, every article has a hook. This has a hook. The hook is that it's a motherfucking ghost train. It's that simple. You could guess almost the entirety of this article from the title and the description on the featured blurb alone, because this entire article lays out everything it does very plainly and matter-of-factly. I'm sure you could conjure up a backstory, some kind of arbitrary "hook" to connect this to in a tale, but because of how well this is written and how this avoids most pitfalls of "thing what does a thing" articles by not actually being boring or uninteresting, it doesn't need any of that. I'd say this article might almost be better off without one.

True, it's not for everyone, but I have to flat-out disagree with both "there generally needs to be some kind of hook - something creepy or wtf about it", and "if it was a new SCP it would… not survive long". On the contrary, if this article exactly were posted today (provided it didn't exist already ofc) it'd survive pretty fairly well. As for something that NEEDS to be creepy or wtf, I'm sure that's personal preference, but an article doesn't need that. In fact:

Because SCP-1489 appears entirely solid and real to direct human observation, it typically causes significant disturbances when traveling through inhabited areas.

Attempts have been made to communicate with SCP-1489's passengers using vehicles synchronized to its movement; however, while passengers have been observed to break off conversation when observed in this fashion, they have so far proven unwilling to communicate with researchers.

What's not creepy or wtf about that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it was a new SCP it would, I think, not survive long. There generally needs to be some hook - something creepy or wtf about it. Here it's literally nothing more than an incorporeal train. Maybe the passengers should be constantly tortured or something, I dunno. That example is hella cliché, don't use it. =P

It's definitely well-written, it just doesn't provide the kind of tone I would expect, so I'm in the "eh" camp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Paranatural Anyone?… (http://www.paranatural.net/index.php?id=227)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I agree with some of the others that perhaps it lacks a little bit of a sting you expect from an SCP this is still a very well written entry. I think the fact the passengers are reactive to outside events but don't seem distressed or confused seems like the creepiest part and what really makes it an SCP. Certainly it's a great setup for future tales: Just why aren't these people upset? Are they traveling through time as well as space? And what are they waiting for?

+1

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noted. It won't let me delete it but if a mod wanted to I understand it crosses a line. For what it's worth, I apologise for being offensive. Live and learn, I guess

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Dankaar's right. If you don't like something, you don't need demand to know why the author didn't make an effort to satisfy you.

Also, if you read through the other comments on the page, it's evident that others find it stimulating enough to like it. There's nothing wrong with not liking something, but making personal snipes at the author is wholly unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Err… Think that might be crossing the line for reviews…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, if this isn't the most boring scp I've ever laid eyes on. The structure and terminology's all solid; But would it have killed you to attempt something even halfway stimulating?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree; the traditional 'Ghost Train' runs between specific points, and is visible only under certain circumstances. An example is the Funeral train that runs from DC to Springfield, IL, on (or around) the anniversary of Lincoln's death — it is not seen by all, watches/clocks/etc., stop as it goes by, and it's only around for a short while during a specific time each year.

This, on the other hand, as befits an SCP, obeys some kind of scientifically definable (even if not understood) rules, and its behavior is consistent and predictable, within the parameters of those rules. We may not understand where it came from, nor why it obeys its rules, but they are consistent, and its behavior may be accurately predicted using the identified rules.

Were it just a 'True-blue completely straight depiction of a ghost train' there would be no controlling / predicting /etc.

Incidentally, I would have guessed it's origin to have been the Salisbury Rail crash in 1906, but that was an L12 class, not a Minerva. (Not easily mistaken for each other, either; the L12 is a 4-4-0, the Minerva a 2-4-0.) There were a few LSWR crashes in the late 1800s, but the Salisbury crash had the most loss of life.

*Disclaimer — I've been working for a Railroad for 17 years; I've heard a LOT of train stories, legends, myths, and so forth.*

Upvoted :)

item.title: Nice. Not awesome, but nice.
item.content:encoded:
It does exactly what it says on the tin. It is an anomaly. Fits all the standard criteria. It's a bit boring to read since there are no real clues what's going on. The earliest recorded major (fatal) accident on the cited line was 1880, so I still don't have a good clue about the source of the ghost train.

My issue is with containment. It would be far more effective/redundant in containment, if we have 9km+repair materials of "old track", to lay out concentric circles, with the train on the interior circle. Any breach would send the train on a tangent course, which should be picked up by the next circle.

Checking specs for applicable train curve radii gives a diameter of the inner loop at a maximum of 320m for the inner loop… possibly as low as 80m. That's roughly 1 km of track. you could build at least 2 loops around that for redundant enclosure out of the existing track.

I'd argue in favor of making a small loop - for ease of observation and potential communication. Also, the "monotony", if such can be applied to ghosts, might give them incentive to communicate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. +1.

What if it's doing one of its around-the-world trips and its trajectory takes it headlong into a manmade solid obstruction that was constructed before 1860 (like, say, the Great Wall of China)? Does it still go through it? Or smash it and do actual physical damage?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
GHOST TRAIIN
This is one of my favorite types of skips. What with all the pushing of "STORY STORY STORY!" nowadays in Series III, I always feel a rush of relief when I finish an article like this and don't find some tacked-on and hackneyed Note From the Creator of the Skip(TM). It's a good old fashioned anomaly; unexplainable, mysterious and just plain intriguing. I'm all for a compelling backstory or pages of exploration logs and explanatory sections, but sometimes you just need some mystery. It's such a simple object, presented in such a way that there are dozens of different explanations and ideas that could stem from it; it doesn't restrict itself to one clearly defined interpretation. Had there been a concrete origin of purpose to this train, it wouldn't have been half as good.
Heartily +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This would make for a great—actually, I can't think of much that isn't added to by ghost train. Like, you might have to say "Fuck you" to Chekhov and his gun, but the aesthetic of an old school, steam powered passenger train can just add so much to the atmosphere of a story. Like, imagine seeing this when you've survived a Type K event? Heartbreaking. Or a kid during the holidays? Polar motherfucking Express, anyone? Ghost train is best train.

Also, I swear I read this before it was featured. No idea why I didn't upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice to see this on the front page, I hope it gets some more recognition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this thing. It's basically a boring oddity that the Foundation has to take care of because they feel obliged to. It's a mediocre enough concept to be interesting. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason I'm imagining that when they get to the last bits of track they'll point them up and let it fly into space

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is that even remotely relevant? MTFs can and will appear across multiple articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 except that "Train-Spotters" was already used on 17 Nov 2011 with SCP-1017. Other than that it is good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Already mentioned above, but I don't see why this is supposed to be Euclid. Sounds like a Safe SCP for me.

Is it really just because of the somewhat awkward containment measures? Still… that doesn't seem adequate. I mean, the thing doesn't even interact with(not counting the tracks… sort of) or harm anything in the real world apart from being visible. I thought Euclid is supposed to mean some degree of danger.

Sure, there might be some unpredictability if the crew/passengers are presumed sentient, but there aren't any documented occasions or proofs of them, are they? If such an event does occur then I guess Euclid might be justified, but as is I'm not convinced…

Also, has anybody tried stopping the train with railway components from said time period? Like those stopper things that were sometimes placed on old rails. Seems like an experiment the Foundation would attempt with something like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One little nitpick: the first sentence of the description doesn't seem necessary. It just sits there by itself, and says the same same thing as the next few sentences

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason, the words "exit trajectory" were amusing to me. No idea why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But… it's not a twist. This is a true-blue completely straight depiction of a ghost train. There is nothing about this that is anything other than a ghost train. This is lifted directly from the words 'ghost' and 'train' and nothing else. This is just a train that is also a ghost. Or a ghost that happens to be a train, if you prefer.

What I'm getting at here is, downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, and also like that it's Euclid. Upvoted! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've never quite understood why people append "Downvoted." or "Upvoted." for that matter. It's not useful to the staff, it's not useful to the author, and it smacks of saying "No I don't like this" and slamming the door on one's face.

hm, I do it because sometimes I actually upvote SCPs that I have criticism for, or I don't vote on them. And for that matter, sometimes I downvote SCPs that have elements I like because I really want to encourage the author to change them. In these contexts, if the author cares about their vote, it's useful to clarify. And if the author doesn't care, no harm no foul.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just wonder if anyone's tried to suplex it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Euclid also makes sense if the crew/passengers are sentient… Same reason most humanoid SCPs, even begin ones, are Euclid— free will and intelligence implies unpredictability.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You downvoted me? How dare you.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You certainly seem to answer your own questions alot. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does it need to be useful to the author/staff? No.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've never quite understood why people append "Downvoted." or "Upvoted." for that matter. It's not useful to the staff, it's not useful to the author, and it smacks of saying "No I don't like this" and slamming the door on one's face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's just what I was thinking. I kinda had this mental conversation:
"It's a ghost train."
"Yes, but what's INTERESTING about it?"
"… It's a freaking ghost train that will only obey really old tracks, otherwise it goes off on its own."
"… Good point."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh.

To Pata H:

Sorry, I didn't mean to be rude or unhelpful about it. I might not be the most polite commenter sometimes, I understand that, sorry for stepping on any toes. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I actually upvoted this for the containment procedures and disinformation campaign. When you get right down to it, it's just a ghost train, and there's maybe a little room for something else. But this is one article where lack of a real hook doesn't hold it back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My thinking had just been that it's ridiculously finicky about its track. It's apparently ok with the historic bits being coated in resin, but as soon as you have a rainstorm or some random animal digs up a bit of it the train "derails" and goes off on a round-the-world adventure which you have to have someone physically follow it on so you don't lose track of it. The fact that it's just going to barrel through any obstacles in its way makes it really hard to keep up with too.

I'm thinking that the thing I need to do is to go into more detail on the passengers. They've presumably seen quite a bit in their time as incorporeal globetrotters…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Euclid would imply that the train sometimes does things we don't expect. The document doesn't address that but part of me likes the idea that there's something we don't know and isn't being said that makes this Euclid.

(which is not much of a defense but WELP)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a thought: Shouldn't this be considered a "Safe" SCP? It's containment is fully understood and straightforward. It also poses no direct threat to life or property in the event of a containment breach. Admittedly, recontainment would prove problematic for the simple logistics (time/effort required to build track, acquisition of suitable resources) but they're all issues of a mundane nature.

Other than that, I like it. +1 from me.

item.title: Re: Yep, I'm back.
item.content:encoded: Oh good, my eyes are safe from your radiance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm considering. I've had some ideas for ways to take this further (particularly in regards to its history as an anomalous object), but I'll admit I like keeping these things short and to the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: LOUUUUD NOIIIISES!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pata, one of the people in this thread is being disruptive and unhelpful and it's not Soulless.

item.title: Re: Yep, I'm back.
item.content:encoded: it's okay to write a good tale for good reasons

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there's potential here. But it needs something else: a creepy origin story, a creepy interaction account, etc. I'm not asking you to "give away" the whole mystery—just to add a little bit of narrative.

Vote withheld, for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apparently he thinks you were trying to be rude/superior/mean/whatever by downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's actually my third - I did 871 and 988 also.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great for a first, assuming it's a first because I didn't see any others on your profile. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great! Good job! You sure showed him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally find these things more frightening when there's no explanation at all. Stuff that just is has always been more scary to me. And presumably the passengers know what's up, but they'e not telling.

I did leave a minor hint with the date range and the train company though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is that they have to have been "originally placed" before 1860. Apparently whatever decides that this train will interact with a track dosn't mind if you move that track around, but it won't accept bits of track that have been modified beyond simple translation.

It's admittedly something of a practicality hack, but it makes some sense if you consider that there's such a thing as "movable track" in the first place, such as roundhouses and switches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing too fancy, I can dig it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice twist on the ghost train concept. Upvoted.
